items.question: What is the difference between SGD and mini-batch gradient descent?
items.answer: Pure SGD uses batch size 1 (one example per gradient). Mini-batch gradient descent uses 1 < B < N. Both are stochastic methods; modern practice often calls mini-batch training "SGD" when using optimizers like `torch.optim.SGD`.

items.question: Why shuffle the training data each epoch in SGD?
items.answer: Shuffling breaks correlation in the order of examples so each mini-batch is a more representative random sample. Without shuffling, batches can be biased (e.g. all one class), skewing gradients and slowing convergence.

items.question: Is the mini-batch gradient biased or unbiased with respect to the full-batch gradient?
items.answer: Unbiased under uniform random sampling: E[∇L_B(θ)] = ∇L(θ). It is noisy—variance scales roughly as 1/B for independent samples—so individual steps deviate from the true gradient direction.

items.question: How does increasing batch size affect the number of updates per epoch and gradient noise?
items.answer: Larger B reduces updates per epoch (about N/B steps) and lowers gradient variance. Smaller B increases updates and noise. There is a trade-off between statistical accuracy per step and exploration frequency.
